speaker: interviewer
text: Leizer Hoffer. During the three and a half years that you were in Shargorod Transnistria, are you aware of any communication at all with the people back home with the Romanian Jews, community that was not deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard that there was definitely an effort by the Romanian Jewish community to help. What form the communication took place, I don't know. Ourselves, like my family, are the people that-- divisions of people had no-- nobody to connect with. But, in general terms, we knew that there was an effort by the Jewish community in Romania, and even maybe by some Jewish people from far away to help. That's all we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we're back at the partisans, you were talking about before.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were starting to hope that maybe a liberation is near. That was in the-- the end of the winter of 1944. As I said, there were signs of some people retreating. The papers started talking about the Russians breaking through on many fronts. The partisan situation started to become more [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] active.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of activity. We heard of partisans attacking there, and partisans attacking here, and the odd partisan falling into the Romanian hands. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And, so [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] all of a sudden, the evacuation of the Germans started to be more intense. The odd convoy would rush through town. And they even had some dead soldiers in the wagons. And they would rush through town, very, very rapidly. We didn't see-- I don't remember seeing too many Romanians retreating, because the Romanian army wasn't active on that front. The Romanian army was more concentrated, I think, in the Odessa area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were-- who were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans and Ukrainians. And they started to retreat and started to retreat. And I don't know how long it took. I think it was early-- it most have been early 1944. It must've been either the end of February. I don't remember for sure. In my mind, it's a long time. It's 50-some years. But suddenly, the same kind of quiet that we encountered in Vijnita. It's an eerie quiet. It's hard to describe. It's-- it's like they say, you can hear the silence. There's nothing. And I remember, it was about 10:00 in the morning, after the third day of quiet, were-- you would still see the odd-- the odd German. I remember a truck stopping and burning all their stuff. They had many, many clothes, and boots, and everything new, brand new. And they burned it all. And then they took off. And at 10 o'clock in the morning, on about the third day of this eerie quiet, we heard machine gun fire. And I remember we were in the house where we had the bunker. We were in that house with some other people in our family and the family we lived with. I don't know, we went there I guess maybe to hide-- I don't remember. But I do remember, suddenly, out of nowhere, Russian soldiers. Pulling machine guns behind them in terrible condition-- torn uniforms, not shaven. It looked like they never slept for weeks. That was the front line. They just passed by our house, marched on. And then people started moving around town freely. And I remember, my younger brother Joe and myself, we started walking in to the town and walking towards the area which headquartered the Romanian authorities. Because the Romanians were gone. They disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Russian soldiers, tanks, cannons, moving constantly. Overhead, airplanes, and the sirens. Apparently, the Germans decided to bomb. But instead of bombing us, they overthrew Shargorod and bombed another village. We heard the bombs dropping. It wasn't very far-- maybe 10, 15 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know the name of that village?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name. I don't remember the name. But I do remember seeing, after about a couple of hours, wagons with wounded Russian soldiers. When the bombs fell, they wounded many Russian soldiers. Some of them were dead. They were carted in wagons by horses. The blood was pouring out of the wagons. I saw that, my own eyes. But that's not-- this is-- I'm just relating that. The story that I will never forget-- as we approached the center where the authorities were, I remember a Russian general [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stood up on a platform and started to address the people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he started to speak in Yiddish. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he said that he is from a certain city in Russia, which he had liberated, and didn't find anybody alive of his family or any other Jewish people. And that our area was the first area where he started seeing Jewish people and recognized Jewish people. And he said that all in Yiddish. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my brother and I, we listened. And immediately, of course, some Ukrainian population started protesting to the fact that he spoke Yiddish and told him [RUSSIAN]. Well, he then started speaking in Russian, but he told us what we wanted to hear. I'll never forget that, because having survived such a tragedy, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] words like that brought us encouragement and gave us some hope. And told us that maybe the Jewish people will survive after all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so, we got liberated that early spring. And within six weeks, we started making plans. We heard that the Russian army had advanced far enough for us to return home. And so one day, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period, after the Russians came, was it easier with food and-- did it get better? Did you get clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, it was-- it started to be easier. People found a little bit more strength in themselves. And I remember this-- I remember somehow that, between all of us, we had a chance to eat the first piece of meat that we'd seen for a long, long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They butchered a cow. And everybody in our area was given, by the people that did that. You know, it's a long story. But we-- I remember having some meat. It was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: We never thought we would ever see food again. When you're hungry like that, the worst fear is-- when you're really hungry-- when will you eat again? Or will you ever eat again? So, where did you get food now, after the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, the Ukrainians started to come in. And I think that people started to get organized a little better to help each other. I really don't remember. I don't remember. But all of a sudden, I do remember that what we did, we bartered. We found that you could buy something here for a certain price, and take it to another town, and sell it for much more money. People started to doing a little trading, and all of a sudden, we had enough food and we started making plans to go home. And we said goodbye to our people that saved our life. Heinach and his wife Ethel, and [NON-ENGLISH], and Yita. They were the only ones that survived-- Shemen died, the older gentleman died. And we said goodbye and we took off on foot. We didn't know-- excuse me-- we didn't know whether we were going to go back to Vijnita, but we heard that there were some people returning to Czernowitz. And-- that's fine. And so, we started towards the general area where we lived before the war. On the way home, we passed Mohyliv-Podilskyi again. I remember walking amongst unexploded bombs, ammunition, abandoned rifles. The bridge was demolished. There was some kind of a provisional--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean from Shargorod to Mogilev you walked back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked all the way. We walked all the way to Mohyliv-Podilskyi walked across that bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there no other way to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know of any other way. And we didn't have anything to pay for any other way. But, on the way home, we did take along a few-- we heard that if you had a bottle of vodka, you might be able to get on a-- on a Russian lorry, a Russian armored tank-- vehicle. So we did get part of the way, we did get a ride.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the vodka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just bought it. The Ukrainians were good at making vodka. So we bought it. I don't know. We bought it. That's what happened. And we did get a ride. And we-- I remember stopping with a local peasant in Bessarabia. When we get back, it's close to Bessarabia, And they gave us food. We didn't have nothing. But they gave us food, we ate with them. They cooked a [NON-ENGLISH] with cheese-- unbelievable. They gave us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, I remember, finally seeing Czernowitz from a distance. You mean you walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. We had part ride and we walked, and we part ride, and we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Took about three, four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Days? Only day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Days and nights. We walked with part ride, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, part ride.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't all walking. You-- it's only-- I don't know how many kilometers, it's not that far, really, in terms of distance. But, anyway, so the outskirts of Czernowitz, beautiful city. I'd never been in a big city. Czernowitz was a big city, big apartments, big houses. And so, one nice day, we marched into Czernowitz, the four of us, in our regs, our sacks, and again, we looked for a place where to put our-- where to find shelter. And indeed, we did find an empty house. And he walked in there, and that's where we decided to make home.

speaker: interviewer
text: This whole way from Shargarod, did you go with other people? Or just the four of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were many people walking. Many people. And we even ran into some young men that joined the Russian army and they, too, were walking. So it was a mixture. Everybody was moving. Everybody was moving. We were going home. Czernowitz wasn't our home, but it was near to our home. As a matter of fact, when we got to Czernowitz, we found out that in Vijnita, there is still a lot of strife going on, that there's still some hooligans hiding out in the forests and the Russians had trouble dealing with them. So we didn't go to Vijnita. We stayed in Czernowitz. I'm going to make this short. In Czernowitz, we settled in, and we tried-- from nothing. We started to be-- to bartering, to exchanging things. Then we got a-- a little-- a little place on the market where we would sell all different kinds of things that we were buying and selling. And somehow we got ourselves into some more respectable clothes. We had food. My dad got a job as a night watchman in a-- in a-- in a Russian military establishment, and a restaurant, and a store. And things got better for us. That lasted for about two years. After two years, the Russian authorities decided that any Romanian citizens that wanted to leave Russia would be free to do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Czernowitz was then in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czernowitz was-- Bukowina was occupied by the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: North Bukowina.

speaker: interviewee
text: North Bukowina. Again, it didn't take us very long to decide that it's time to move on. And we left the Soviet Union and went to Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember crossing the border, and I remember seeing the first Romanian soldier. And I was very frightened. The picture of the Romanian soldier being the killer came back to me, and I questioned, what did we do? We left the Russians and who did we leave them for, for the Romanian killers?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we heard that maybe there was some possibilities to leave Romania as well, for other countries-- if-- if it's possible. So our chances were a little better with the Romanians. Of course, this was after the war already. And so we, by transport, we ended up two or three days later, in a little city in Transylvania called Oradea Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trucks. We arranged for transportation to Oradea Mare. In Oradea Mare, we found accommodations with a family. We rented a couple rooms from them. And in Oradea Mare, there was some Zionist organizations trying to get young Jewish boys into [NON-ENGLISH] camp, preparing them to go to Israel. So both my brother and I registered to do that. Of course, in Russia, after the war, my youngest brother Sam-- Simcha-- was born. He was born in 1945, so my parents decided they wanted to have another child. And, of course, we were very happy about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In retrospect, I give my parents credit. They're both gone-- may they rest in peace. But they found it-- they found it strong enough to bring another-- another baby into the world and hoping maybe that he'd have a better life than what they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's an interesting name they gave him, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they named him after my uncle Simcha that died in Shargarod in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Simcha also means--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we settled in in Oradea Mare. And we started to be aware of the fact that there was no future there for us. By that time, I was 17, 18. My younger brother was 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, one day, I said to my brother, Joe, this is no place for us. There's no future here. What are we going to do? We have no schooling. We haven't learned a trade. Where are we going to go, to the Romanian army? We must leave here and rescue us, ourselves, and maybe have a chance to rescue our parents. Did you not think ever of going back to Vijnita?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did go back once. We did during the two years that we were in Czernowitz, we went back once. And it was the same city without a heart, as I left the day when I mentioned to you that we were going to the station. It was dead. It was dead. It was like a dead tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we confronted our parents one day with the decision that we are going to leave them and go across borders and try to somehow get to either Israel-- or Palestine at that time-- or some other country, where maybe we'll have a chance to get them out. And so we, one night, we said goodbye. It was a terrible feeling for us, and it was worse for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But luckily, we made it to Budapest, and we sent word back to them that we made it. And we understand that after that, a lot of other people followed in our footsteps. In Budapest, we were a short time, and we made it to Austria, to Vienna, and then to Salzburg. And within a year after we left, we left about Pesach time. Within a year after we left, we were in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- From Budapest, there was already organization, the UNRAA, and the Joint, and the [NON-ENGLISH]. The further we went, the more it was organized to help us get out. And then I remember finally getting to Italy. In Italy, we were first in Milano. Then we went to another town, a city in Cremona. In Cremona, we were about six months. We heard that-- we wanted to go to Palestine, but the borders were closed and people couldn't make it. They wouldn't allow. The British wouldn't allow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we heard of an opportunity to go to Canada, and we registered, my brother and I, to go to Canada. And we were accepted. And one day we were told to take the shots, and we were put on a boat called We left Genoa, and on the 22nd of March, 1948, we landed in Halifax, Nova Scotia.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went on to Montreal. And from Montreal, we went to an area in Saskatchewan, which is called Hoffer, Saskatchewan-- which we understand-- understood at that time, there was some distant relative of ours that had lived on a farm colony. So that's where we ended up, in Hoffer, Saskatchewan. We worked with-- with those people. They weren't our relatives, but they were from the same general area as we were, and their name was Hoffer. They treated us not like relatives, but they gave us work, paid us a minimum wage, helped maybe us getting our parents out. For that, I'm always going to be grateful to them. To this day, we are still feel like kinship. And I certainly am thankful for whatever they did to help us. But then my brother and I stuck out on our own. We ventured into-- first we found jobs. We moved to Winnipeg. We found jobs in Winnipeg. Then we started in the cattle business. And we struggled on with life. We were very young. We had big responsibilities. We wanted to get our parents out. So not only did we have to care for ourselves, but we had to care for them and for our younger brother, Sam. And thanks God, we got them out-- via Israel, but we got them out. And life was very difficult, being without money, and without a trade, and without anybody to really help us, and having to carry that burden. But we carried on. We were fighters. And thanks God, now that I look back, we-- we got married. My younger brother finished his schooling. Unfortunately, while we were in the-- on the farm, we had a terrible accident that my dad, having survived a war and having survived a concentration camp, died in a tractor accident. My dad had a very difficult time adjusting. He was a broken man, like I said from before. He lived in a strange country, strange language, no friends, no acquaintances. He couldn't do anything to help. He felt he was lost. But unfortunately, like I said, there was a terrible tragedy. He died. So after he died, we left that area and my mother and my younger brother lived with us for some time. My youngest brother Sam was a very determined man, a young man. He wanted to get an education. Worked hard, got an education, then they moved to Ottawa and my mother went with him. And he provided for my mother and got a good job with the government. We also prospered in some way. We got married, raised families, raised our own children. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all I can say is this story only tells part of the struggle and of the suffering we went through. The scars will never heal. But our job is to tell our story so that our children and our children's children will know what happened to us and the price we paid. And that our message for them is the message that we read on the wall in Atachi-- don't ever forget. Don't ever forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I'd like to introduce my wife, Maggie. Maggie is also a Holocaust survivor. She was born in Hungary, survived the war, lost both of her parents. I was lucky enough to find her in Winnipeg. We got married. We raised four beautiful children. We just had our first grandchild. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, good. Nice to hear after 36 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my uncle Simcha, who died in Shargorod of typhus-- my dad's brother. This is my aunty Henia. She was my dad's younger sister. I believe 18 years old, or 20, at the most, during the war. Raped and murdered by the Nazis, somewhere on the River Bug. This is my dad's parents, my grandparents. My grandmother, Vicky, and my grandfather, Yehosia-- who were also murdered at the same time as my aunt Henia, on the rivers-- on the borders of the River Bug, somewhere in Transnistria. I don't know what town. Our children, Anthony and Gailia-- the proud parents of a baby girl, our first grandchild. Our only daughter, our first born, Michelle, and her husband, Sam. We're very proud of them. And these two younger men, to the right is our youngest son, Mark, and our son Gary-- two good-looking bachelors. I hope they find some girls soon.
